questions.question: Define ε-NFA and explain the significance of epsilon transitions.
keyPoints: ε-NFA is a 5-tuple (Q, Σ, δ, q0, F) where δ: Q × (Σ ∪ {ε}) → P(Q)
Epsilon transitions allow movement without consuming input
Provides flexibility in automaton design
Simplifies construction of complex automata

questions.question: What is epsilon closure? How is it computed?
keyPoints: Epsilon closure of state q is set of all states reachable via zero or more ε-transitions
Always includes the state q itself
Algorithm: Start with {q}, add states reachable via ε-transitions, repeat until no new states

questions.question: Show that ε-NFA and NFA are equivalent in computational power.
keyPoints: Both accept exactly regular languages
To convert ε-NFA to NFA: for each state q and input a, compute ε-closure(δ(ε-closure(q), a))
This eliminates ε-transitions while preserving language
Proof via construction shows equivalence

questions.question: Explain the procedure to convert ε-NFA to DFA using subset construction.
keyPoints: Each DFA state represents a set of ε-NFA states
Start state is ε-closure of original start state
For each DFA state S and input a, compute transition to ε-closure of union of δ(s, a) for all s in S
Final states are those containing at least one original final state

questions.question: Draw and explain the transition diagram for ε-NFA accepting (a|b)*ab.
keyPoints: Include states for handling Kleene star prefix
Use ε-transitions to connect components
Must show proper start and final states
ε-transitions allow looping for (a|b)*

questions.question: What are the advantages of using ε-transitions in automaton design?
keyPoints: Modular design - easier to combine smaller automata
Represents optional behavior elegantly
Simplifies complex transition logic
Useful for modeling real-world scenarios with optional steps

questions.question: Prove that ε-NFA accepts only regular languages.
keyPoints: ε-NFA can be converted to NFA
NFA can be converted to DFA
DFA accepts regular languages
Therefore ε-NFA accepts regular languages only

questions.question: Construct an ε-NFA for the regular expression (a|b)*abb. Show the transition diagram and explain how it accepts the string 'ababb'.
keyPoints: Draw states: q0 (start), q1, q2, q3 (final)
ε-transitions for Kleene star handling
Transitions: q0 --a/b--> q0 (loop), q0 --a--> q1, q1 --b--> q2, q2 --b--> q3
Path for 'ababb': q0(ε)→q0--a-->q1--b-->q2--b-->q3 (stuck), retry: q0--a-->q1, q1--b-->q2, q2--b-->q3 accepts 'abb' but not 'ababb'
Correct construction handles longer strings

questions.question: Convert the following ε-NFA to NFA: Q={q0,q1,q2}, Σ={a,b}, F={q2}, δ: δ(q0,a)={q1}, δ(q0,ε)={q1}, δ(q1,b)={q2}, δ(q1,ε)={q2}. Show all steps of conversion.
keyPoints: Compute ε-closure: ε-closure(q0)={q0,q1,q2}, ε-closure(q1)={q1,q2}, ε-closure(q2)={q2}
For NFA δ': δ'(q0,a)=ε-closure(δ(ε-closure(q0),a))=ε-closure(δ({q0,q1,q2},a))
δ'(q0,b)=ε-closure(δ(ε-closure(q0),b))=ε-closure(δ({q0,q1,q2},b))
Final NFA transition table with states q0,q1,q2

questions.question: Explain the algorithm to convert ε-NFA to DFA. Apply this algorithm to convert an ε-NFA with 2 states to equivalent DFA.
keyPoints: Algorithm steps: 1) Find ε-closure of all states 2) Start state = ε-closure(start) 3) For each state S in DFA and input a, compute new state = ε-closure(δ(S,a)) 4) Mark final states
Apply to example: Let ε-NFA have q0(start), q1(final), ε-transition from q0 to q1
ε-closure(q0)={q0,q1}, ε-closure(q1)={q1}
DFA start: {q0,q1}, for input a: δ({q0,q1},a)=∅, so transition to ∅
For input b: δ({q0,q1},b)=∅
Final DFA states: any containing q1
